justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified as a domain model change — business_context calls it 'a classic cross-context integration challenge' and the entire analysis treats it as creating a new integration seam, not a simple feature add. (2) Data ownership clearly raised — BR-2 declares Context A as sole owner of StockLevel, and a dedicated escalation asks which context should own the new Stock Reservation concept. (3) TOCTOU identified — AC-7 tests two simultaneous orders for the last unit, edge case 'Two concurrent orders compete for the last unit' explicitly requires concurrency controls, and the eventually-consistent indicator edge case captures the read-then-act race. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — 'Stock Reservation' is defined as a NEW concept not in either context today, and the confidence assumptions explicitly contrast 'Stock Reservation pattern vs. synchronous stock check with no reservation.' (5) Escalation to MJ present — four well-structured escalations covering block semantics, staleness SLA, fail-open/fail-closed policy, and reservation ownership, all of which are architectural coupling decisions. (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-6 (reservations must not exceed StockLevel) and BR-3 (order blocking when available stock insufficient) are both new invariants created by the integration.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough treatment of the Stock Reservation concept — defined it as new, identified its lifecycle (create, confirm, expire, release), flagged expiry as critical to prevent Available Stock deadlock, and escalated ownership.
TOCTOU addressed at multiple levels: the reservation mechanism itself is the solution, AC-7 tests the race condition, and the edge case about indicator staleness captures the UI-level TOCTOU.
Data ownership question explored beyond the obvious StockLevel ownership — the escalation about which context owns Stock Reservations shows deep domain thinking with three concrete options.
Rejection reasons are excellent — directly ties violations back to business rules, including the subtle deadlock scenario where reservation leaks silently block all orders.
Risk assessment correctly identifies the two highest risks: reservation leaks degrading Available Stock to zero, and false certainty from eventually consistent indicators.
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the number of genuinely undefined policy decisions (fail-open/closed, partial fulfillment, block semantics, staleness SLA).

Minor: escalations could more explicitly name MJ as the target for the architectural coupling pattern decision, though the escalation type and content make it clear these are architecture-level questions.

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A request to integrate two previously independent bounded contexts — Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management — so that order placement becomes coupled to real-time stock availability. This is a classic cross-context integration challenge where the boundary between inventory truth and sales workflow must be carefully negotiated.","bounded_context":"Cross-cutting concern spanning Warehouse Management (Context A) and Sales/Order Management (Context B)

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified as a domain model change — the business_context calls it 'fundamentally changes the autonomy of the Sales context', the contradiction escalation flags the existing design explicitly decoupled the contexts and this feature removes that autonomy. (2) Data ownership clearly raised — BR-1 states 'StockLevel is owned exclusively by Context A', ubiquitous language defines Availability Indicator as a read-only projection distinct from the authoritative StockLevel. (3) TOCTOU race condition identified — BR-4 explicitly states 'A point-in-time stock check is a classic race condition — two orders could both see stock=1 and both proceed', AC-4 tests the concurrent-order scenario. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — BR-4 and BR-5 draw a sharp line between the reservation mechanism (for blocking) and the eventually-consistent indicator (for display), with BR-5 marked invariant. (5) Escalation for architectural coupling — three well-structured escalations cover partial availability policy, real-time SLA impossibility, and the fundamental contradiction with the existing decoupled design, each with options and recommendations. (6) New cross-context invariants stated — BR-1 through BR-7 define the integration's invariants, with clear invariant/non-invariant classification and justifications.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on the TOCTOU problem — not only identified but translated into a concrete business rule (BR-4), a testable acceptance criterion (AC-4), and a rejection reason when the reservation mechanism is absent.
The distinction between the availability indicator (eventually consistent, display-only) and the reservation mechanism (authoritative, for blocking) is exactly the nuance the scenario demands.

The contradiction escalation is particularly strong — it quotes the existing spec ('Orders are placed by customers without checking Context A') and frames the new feature as a direct contradiction, not just an addition.

Edge cases are well-chosen and business-relevant: partial availability, damaged goods during picking, payment failure after reservation, and unknown-to-warehouse products all surface real integration pitfalls.
Rejection reasons tie specific spec violations to specific business rules, giving downstream agents clear justification for pushback.

BR-3 (Sales must not require synchronous Warehouse availability) is a subtle but critical invariant that many analyses would miss — it preserves the original architectural intent while allowing integration.
Escalations do not explicitly name MJ as the target, though the escalation type and content clearly imply architectural review. Minor gap given the escalations are substantively correct.

This is a cross-bounded-context integration that fundamentally changes the autonomy of the Sales context and introduces a hard coupling between two previously independent domains.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Orde

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified as a domain model change — the analysis frames this as 'significant architectural and domain shift' coupling two previously independent bounded contexts, with BR-6 enforcing anti-corruption layers. (2) Data ownership question raised — analysis asks 'WHO owns the truth about stock availability' and distinguishes StockLevel (Context A) from Available Stock (undefined). (3) TOCTOU/race condition identified — edge case 'Last-unit race condition' and BR-3/BR-4 explicitly address concurrent orders seeing the same stock and overselling. AC-4 tests this directly. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — 'Stock Reservation' is called out as 'NOT YET DEFINED' in ubiquitous language, BR-4 distinguishes Available Stock from StockLevel, and escalation #3 explicitly frames the missing reservation concept. (5) Escalation to MJ for architectural coupling pattern — four detailed escalations cover the integration pattern, staleness requirements, reservation mechanism, and degradation strategy. (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-2 defines the order-blocking invariant, BR-4 defines the Available Stock computation invariant, and BR-6 defines the context boundary invariant.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough identification of the TOCTOU problem — not just mentioned but modeled with concrete edge cases (last-unit race) and a dedicated acceptance criterion (AC-4)
Stock Reservation explicitly called out as 'NOT YET DEFINED' in the ubiquitous language, with a dedicated escalation explaining why it's required and offering three concrete alternatives
Data ownership cleanly separated — StockLevel belongs to Warehouse, Available Stock is a derived concept requiring reservation tracking, and the stock indicator is an eventually-consistent read model
Anti-corruption layer requirement (BR-6) prevents the failure mode of Sales directly reading Warehouse aggregates

Goes beyond rubric requirements by identifying additional concerns: degradation strategy when Warehouse is unavailable, ambiguity around what 'block' means, and the impossibility of true real-time cross-context reads
Rejection reasons section explicitly calls out three ways the feature request as written would violate domain integrity — strong domain authority stance
Low confidence score (45) is well-calibrated given the high ambiguity in the feature request, with clear separation of high-confidence vs low-confidence areas
